settings.autoSaveFailedDetailBody: Your changes are still on this screen and have not been written locally yet. Fix the issue, then use Save now to retry. Reason: {{error}}
settings.fixValidationErrors: Fix the highlighted settings before saving
settings.closeSaveBlocked: Could not close yet because some settings still need attention

overview.title: Get YAT into a truly usable state
overview.desc: Dial in speech capture, polishing, permissions, and output flow first. Once those are solid, every recording feels natural instead of fragile.

setup.title: Highest-impact next steps
setup.desc: Keep the focus on four things that make or break the experience: speech input, output mode, text rules, and platform permissions.
setup.speechPending: You still need a complete STT endpoint and model configuration.
setup.outputTitle: Choose how aggressive output should feel
setup.outputAutoPaste: Results will be pasted into the focused app.
setup.outputClipboard: Results will only be copied to the clipboard.
setup.promptReady: You already have {{count}} vocabulary rules or custom instructions.
setup.promptPending: You are still on the default rules, which is fine for now.
setup.promptOptional: Polishing is off, so prompt work can wait.

permissions.title: Platform permissions & edge cases
permissions.desc: Microphone access, background hotkeys, and auto-paste do not behave the same on every platform. Know the sharp edges before they surprise you.
permissions.macosMicrophoneBody: If you denied microphone access the first time, reopen it under System Settings → Privacy & Security → Microphone.
permissions.macosAccessibilityBody: Auto-paste usually requires YAT to be allowed under System Settings → Privacy & Security → Accessibility.
permissions.windowsMicrophoneBody: Open Settings → Privacy & security → Microphone and confirm desktop apps are allowed to use the mic.
permissions.windowsAutoPasteBody: If the target app runs elevated and YAT does not, simulated paste can fail. Clipboard-only mode is usually the safest fallback.
permissions.linuxMicrophoneBody: Make sure your desktop environment, package format, or portal setup allows microphone access.
permissions.linuxHotkeyBody: YAT currently uses an X11-based global hotkey listener. In native Wayland sessions, hotkeys may not fire at all unless your environment provides compatibility. Linux builds also do not currently consume Esc away from the foreground app during an active operation.
permissions.unknownBody: If recording, hotkeys, or paste behavior feels unreliable, check microphone, keyboard monitoring, and accessibility-related permissions first.

recent.title: Latest pipeline status
recent.desc: Check where the workflow is right now, and surface errors as soon as they happen.
recent.emptyDesc: Record a short sample and the latest result will show up here.

prompt.title: Prompt & vocabulary preview
prompt.desc: Seeing the final combined rules matters. It tells you what the model is actually getting, not just what you think you configured.
prompt.enabledHint: These rules will directly shape the polished output.
prompt.disabledHint: Polishing is disabled, so these rules are currently inactive.

general.singleDesc: Press once to start, press again to stop.
general.doubleTapDesc: Trigger by hitting the same key twice quickly.
general.holdDesc: Only record while the key is held down.
general.keyHint: Examples: RCtrl, RCmd, Alt, F8, Space, A. Use supported key names exactly.
general.modifierHint: Examples: Ctrl, Alt, Shift, or Cmd.
general.doubleTapHint: Shorter is harder to trigger accidentally, but also demands faster tapping.
general.hotkeyPreview: You will use {{hotkey}} to trigger recording.
general.hotkeyBehaviorHint: All four trigger modes stay available. You can use Alt as hold-to-talk now and later switch it to single key, double-tap, or combo. Safer defaults only change what new setups start with.
general.hotkeyRecommendedBody: Hold-to-talk on {{recommended}} is the safest default. It keeps accidental triggers low and usually stays out of the foreground app's way.
general.hotkeyComboBody: Combos are the next safest option. On supported platforms, YAT can consume the action key after the combo is recognized, so stray characters are less likely to leak into the foreground app.
general.hotkeyModifierHoldBody: Hold-to-talk on Alt / Ctrl / Cmd is supported, but those keys sit closer to app and OS shortcuts than {{recommended}}. If you like Alt hold today, you can still switch the same key to single key or another mode later — just test it in the apps you actually use.
general.hotkeyModifierRiskBody: Single or double-tap modifier keys can still affect app menus, OS shortcuts, or focus behavior before YAT knows whether you meant the hotkey. Prefer hold-to-talk on {{recommended}} or a combo instead.
general.hotkeyTypingRiskBody: Using letters, Space, Tab, or Enter as a standalone hotkey steals normal typing. Only keep this if you truly want that key dedicated to YAT.
general.hotkeyCustomBody: This setup can work, but you should test it in the apps you actually use. The safest patterns are hold-to-talk on {{recommended}} or a combo you never need for normal typing.
general.hotkeyWindowPauseBody: When the settings window is focused, YAT pauses recording hotkeys automatically so editing fields does not accidentally start dictation.
general.recordingHotkeyBody: Hold a key for over 0.4 s → hold-to-talk; press two keys together → combo; tap twice quickly → double-tap; short single press → single trigger.
general.recordingWaitingKeys: Waiting for key input (press Esc to cancel)
general.hotkeyValidationMissingKey: Enter a key name first. Escape is reserved for cancel, so choose another key.
general.hotkeyValidationEscapeReserved: Escape is reserved for cancelling recordings and cannot be used as the hotkey or modifier.
general.hotkeyValidationUnsupportedKey: Use a supported key name: a single character, F1–F12, Alt, Ctrl, Shift, Cmd / Meta, Space, Tab, CapsLock, Backspace, or Enter.
general.hotkeyValidationMissingModifier: Combo mode needs both a main key and a modifier.
general.hotkeyValidationUnsupportedModifier: Use a supported modifier name such as Ctrl, Alt, Shift, Cmd, or another supported single key.
general.hotkeyValidationSameKey: The combo key and modifier must be different.
general.hotkeyValidationDoubleTapRange: Double-tap interval must stay between 100ms and 1000ms.
general.autoStartHint: Let YAT wait in the background as soon as you sign in.
general.startMinimizedHint: Launch hidden in the system tray instead of showing the main window.
general.closeToTrayHint: Closing the window hides YAT to the system tray instead of quitting.
general.maxRecordingHint: Lower values prevent runaway recordings; higher values work better for long dictation.
general.autoPasteDesc: Fastest when it works, but relies on simulated paste being accepted by the target app.
general.clipboardOnlyDesc: More conservative and easier to trust in permission-sensitive workflows.
general.clipboardBehaviorHint: Decide whether a copy should always remain available after paste attempts.
general.alwaysCopyToggleHint: Keep a copy in clipboard even when paste succeeds.
general.timeoutHint: Cancel requests automatically after this duration.
general.maxRetriesHint: How many times to retry on failure.
general.microphoneHint: If you just plugged in a device, refresh once before assuming it is missing.
general.soundEffectsHint: Helpful when you want confirmation without looking at the screen.
general.autoMuteHint: Helps reduce feedback, but changes how you can listen to other apps while dictating.
general.autoMuteLinuxBody: Auto-mute is currently a no-op on Linux in this build. YAT still records normally, but it will not mute or restore system audio for you.
general.quickStartDesc: Most people only need a solid hotkey, a clear output mode, and the right microphone. Once those feel right, YAT becomes something you keep using every day.
general.hotkeyDesc: Choose the trigger style that best matches your real work rhythm.
general.recordingDesc: Tune the capture device, duration, and audio feedback so every start and stop feels predictable.
general.outputDesc: This setting changes whether YAT behaves like an eager assistant or a quiet clipboard helper.
general.platformHelpMac: On macOS, microphone and Accessibility permissions are the two most common reasons for broken paste or unreliable background hotkeys.
general.platformHelpWindows: On Windows, auto-paste often fails when the target app runs with higher privileges. Clipboard-only is usually the safest fallback.
general.platformHelpLinux: Linux desktop environments vary a lot in hotkey and simulated keyboard support, so optimize for stability first.
general.platformHelpUnknown: If hotkeys, recording, or paste feel unstable, check system-level permissions before changing app settings.
general.appearanceDesc: Make the app feel native in your desktop environment instead of visually noisy.
general.advancedDesc: Adjust the app for slow networks, background workflows, and longer waits.
general.advancedNote: Timeout and retry settings affect perceived responsiveness. On stable networks, lower retry counts often feel better because failures surface faster.

stt.baseUrlHint: Use the OpenAI-compatible root URL. YAT will append /audio/transcriptions automatically.
stt.apiKeyHintRemote: Remote providers usually require an API key.
stt.apiKeyHintLocal: Optional for localhost or private-network STT endpoints that do not require auth.
stt.quickStartDesc: Pick a stable provider, set the model, add a key if your endpoint requires one, then run a quick connection test.
stt.connectionDesc: Base URL and model always need to be valid. API keys are optional for local no-auth endpoints.
stt.validationDesc: Sends a short silence sample so you can verify the full request path instead of trusting the form blindly.
stt.validationSuccessBody: Your current STT settings responded successfully.

llm.enabledHint: When this is off, YAT outputs the raw transcript directly for maximum speed and predictability.
llm.apiKeyHintRemote: Remote providers usually require an API key.
llm.apiKeyHintLocal: Optional for localhost or private-network LLM endpoints that do not require auth.
llm.quickStartTitle: Polishing is optional, but it should feel solid when enabled
llm.quickStartDesc: Think of polishing as a second-pass quality layer: punctuation, cleanup, formatting, and tone smoothing. Keep it off if speed matters most.
llm.modeDesc: Decide whether YAT should add a cleanup pass after transcription.
llm.connectionDesc: YAT uses the /chat/completions path, so make sure the provider and model support it. API keys are optional for local no-auth endpoints.
llm.bestUseBody: Turn this on when you want filler removal, punctuation, bullet formatting, or a more readable final result.
llm.validationDesc: Sends a tiny request so you can confirm the endpoint and key are both working.
llm.validationSuccessBody: Your current polishing settings responded successfully.
llm.disabledNoticeBody: You can still record and output text normally. Come back later if you want cleaner formatting and smarter polishing.

prompt.userInstructionsDesc: Start with simple rules like “keep the casual tone” or “turn lists into bullets.” Most users never need to edit the system prompt.
prompt.userInstructionsHint: Describe the output style you want before piling on more rules. Clear and compact wins.
prompt.systemPromptDesc: This is the lowest-level and strongest rule source. Only change it when you really know what you want to override.
prompt.llmDisabledBody: Prompt and vocabulary rules only matter when the LLM pass is enabled. If you just want raw speech output, it is fine to leave polishing off for now.
prompt.simpleFirstBody: Projects like OpenTypeless and Whispering handle this well: the best prompt UX does not push everyone into advanced config. It lets you get real value from a few simple instructions first.
prompt.previewTitle: What the model will actually see
prompt.previewDesc: This merges the default system prompt, your custom instructions, and vocabulary rules so you can inspect the final payload instead of guessing.
prompt.advancedWarningTitle: Only edit this when you truly need to change the base strategy
prompt.advancedWarningBody: The system prompt strongly affects every single output. Over-editing it often makes the app worse, not better. Try simple instructions first.

presets.keepToneValue: Keep the original tone. Do not over-formalize the writing.
presets.bulletsValue: If the content contains multiple items, turn them into a bullet list.
presets.formalValue: Make the text cleaner and clearer without changing the meaning.
presets.meetingValue: If the content sounds like tasks or updates, format it like concise meeting notes.

vocabulary.emptyHint: Add your first term to improve accuracy
vocabulary.safeCustomizationTitle: This is the safest customization entry point
vocabulary.safeCustomizationBody: If you mainly want to fix product names, project codes, brand spelling, or team terminology, vocabulary rules are usually safer than editing prompts directly.
vocabulary.addDesc: Each rule becomes part of the polishing pass and helps you build a lightweight personal dictionary.
vocabulary.validationRequired: Please fill in both the wrong form and the correct form.
vocabulary.validationDuplicate: That wrong form already exists, so you do not need to add it again.
vocabulary.listDesc: The more complete this list is, the more stable proper noun correction becomes.

history.emptyHint: Start recording to see results here
history.summaryBody: This should be the place you revisit when you want to copy, retry, compare, or recover good output quickly.

metrics.retentionHint: Entries older than this can be cleaned up regularly.

history.controlsDesc: Retention is part of app settings. Changes save automatically, and you can still save manually if you want immediate confirmation.
history.resultsDesc: Each item should be easy to copy, retry, or delete so good output stays useful and failed output is easy to recover.

capsule.clipboardFallbackHint: Paste failed, but the text is still in your clipboard.
capsule.clipboardFallbackHintWindows: Paste failed, but the text is still in your clipboard. On Windows this often happens when the target app is running elevated or simulated input is blocked.
capsule.clipboardFallbackHintMacos: Paste failed, but the text is still in your clipboard. Make sure YAT has Accessibility permission and the target app accepts simulated input.
capsule.errorUnknown: Something went wrong, but no detailed reason was provided.
